coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive fluorescent probe for selective detection of hypochlorite ions using nitrogen–fluorine co-doped carbon nanodots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Nie, Yujing
Wang, Shuhan
Lin, Youxiu
Lai, Wenqiang
Weng, Wen
Tang, Dianping
coredata.dc:description: Hypochlorite ions (ClO−) are widely used in bleaching agents and disinfectants. However, high concentrations of chloride species are harmful to human health. Therefore, effective methods for the detection of ClO− ions are required. In this study, using 4-fluorophthalic acid and glycine, nitrogen–fluorine co-doped carbon nanodots (N,F-CDs) were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal synthesis for use as a fluorescent probe for the fluorometric detection of ClO− in aqueous media, based on the inhibition of n → π* transitions. The excitation and emission peak centers of the N,F-CDs are at 387 and 545 nm, respectively. The N,F-CDs show a fast quenching response (<1 min) for ClO− and can be used in a wide pH range (pH 4–13). Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity decreased with increase in the ClO− concentration from 0 to 35 μM, and a low limit of detection (9.6 nM) was achieved. This probe possesses excellent selectivity and high sensitivity and was used to analyze standardized samples of piped water, achieving a satisfactory recovery. Thus, this nitrogen–fluorine co-doped nanodot probe is promising for the detection of pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Hypochlorite ions
Fluorescent probe
Nitrogen–fluorine co-doping

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of ClO− in real water samples
Optimization of the N,F-CD-based fluorometric determination system
Detection of ClO− based on the N,F-CDs
Detection of ClO− in real water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Typical TEM image of the N,F-CDs; (B) size distribution of the N,F-CDs; (C) FT-IR spectrum of the N,F-CDs.
(A) XPS spectrum of the obtained N,F-CDs. (B–E) The corresponding C1s, N1s, O1s, and F1s spectra, respectively.
(A) Excitation–emission color map of the N,F-CDs; (B) Absorption (blue curve), excitation (black curve), and fluorescence (FL) spectra (red curve) of the N,F-CDs.

(A) The effect of time of the N,F-CDs on Normalized fluorescence intensity; (B) The effect of NaCl concentrations of the N,F-CDs on Normalized fluorescence intensity; (C) Normalized intensity of N,F-CDs after the addition of various ions. (D) Normalized intensity of ClO−-N,F-CDs after the addition of various ions.

(A) Normalized fluorescence intensity of ClO−-N,F-CDs for different durations. (B) Normalized fluorescence intensity of N,F-CDs with or without ClO− at different pH values; (C) Fluorescence spectra of the N,F-CDs after the addition of different concentrations of ClO− (from top to bottom: 0–35 μM); (D) the relationship between ClO− concentration (0–35 μM) and the fluorescence quenching rate of N,F-CDs.
Determination of ClO− in tap water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive fluorescent probe for selective detection of hypochlorite ions using nitrogen–fluorine co-doped carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology of Fujian Province, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology of Fujian Province
College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology of Fujian Province, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection for Food Safety (Ministry of Education & Fujian Province), Institute of Nanomedicine and Nanobiosensing, Department of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection for Food Safety (Ministry of Education & Fujian Province)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection for Food Safety (Ministry of Education & Fujian Province), Institute of Nanomedicine and Nanobiosensing, Department of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hypochlorite ions (ClO−) are widely used in bleaching agents and disinfectants. However, high concentrations of chloride species are harmful to human health. Therefore, effective methods for the detection of ClO− ions are required. In this study, using 4-fluorophthalic acid and glycine, nitrogen–fluorine co-doped carbon nanodots (N,F-CDs) were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal synthesis for use as a fluorescent probe for the fluorometric detection of ClO− in aqueous media, based on the inhibition of n → π* transitions. The excitation and emission peak centers of the N,F-CDs are at 387 and 545 nm, respectively. The N,F-CDs show a fast quenching response (<1 min) for ClO− and can be used in a wide pH range (pH 4–13). Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity decreased with increase in the ClO− concentration from 0 to 35 μM, and a low limit of detection (9.6 nM) was achieved. This probe possesses excellent selectivity and high sensitivity and was used to analyze standardized samples of piped water, achieving a satisfactory recovery. Thus, this nitrogen–fluorine co-doped nanodot probe is promising for the detection of pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: As a strong oxidizer, hypochlorite ions (ClO−) are often employed in bleaching agents and disinfectants and for chloramine preparation in the pharmaceutical industry [1–4]. For example, compared to the use of liquid-based chlorine, the use of ClO− to disinfect piped water is safe, portable, and inexpensive. However, because of its instability, ClO− cannot be stored in large quantities, so it must be prepared and tested frequently. Further, high concentrations of ClO− can cause pollution. In addition, although the oxygen produced by the decomposition of ClO− can disinfect water, it can also cause respiratory tract diseases and irritate the respiratory mucosa [5,6]. Moreover, excess ClO− can cause oxidative stress in cells, potentially causing diseases such as leukemia, nephritis, tumors, and atherosclerosis [7–9]. Finally, chloroform and other carcinogenic substances can be formed by reactions with other substances in the treated water.

To date, a range of techniques have been employed for the detection of ClO−, including spectrophotometry, surface-enhanced raman scattering, electrochemical detection, and colorimetric biosensors [10–14]. However, most of these methods are time consuming and complicated to perform; further, they have low sensitivity, a narrow linear range, and poor selectivity. Compared with other methods, fluorescence methods are highly sensitive, highly selective, inexpensive, simple, and rapid [15–17]. Therefore, a fast, simple, and highly sensitive probe for the detection of ClO−, especially one based on fluorescence, would be highly practical.

The use of fluorescence probes is crucial in the fluorescence detection method. Several different types of fluorescent probes have been developed so far, for example, organic fluorescent probes [18–20]. However, these are prone to bleaching and have poor stability, limiting their practicality for fluorescence detection. Traditional semiconductor quantum dot probes typically contain heavy metals, and their synthesis is expensive, uses toxic components, and can result in environmental pollution [21–23]. Therefore, carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon dots, have drawn great attention because of their good luminescence performance, non-toxicity, good biocompatibility, good thermal stability, and ease of modification [24–27]. Based on these characteristics, carbon dots are promising fluorescent probes. In this study, we synthesized carbon dots co-doped with nitrogen and fluorine (N,F-CDs) by one-pot hydrothermal synthesis and tested them as probes for the determination of ClO−. In the course of ClO− detection, the obtained N,F-CDs showed excellent sensitivity and selectivity, and thus showed potential for the detection of ClO− in real water samples.

Sodium hypochlorite was purchased from Xilong Chemical Co. Ltd. (Guangdong, China). 4-fluorophthalic acid and glycine were obtained from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Distilled water was used throughout the study. All other reagents were used as received without further purification.

The N,F-CDs were synthesized by modifying our previously reported method [28]. First, 4-fluorophthalic acid (1.5 g, 8.15 mmol) and glycine (1.5 g, 19.98 mmol) were added to a beaker in turn. Then, ultrapure water (35 mL) was added. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 190 °C for 18 h. After the autoclave had cooled, the solution was transferred to a dialysis bag and submerged in ultrapure water for 3 days. The ultrapure water was replaced every 12 h. Finally, the N,F-CD solution was obtained.

A specific concentration of N,F-CDs was obtained with mixing different proportions of ultrapure water. First, the N,F-CD solution was injected into 7-mL PCR tubes. Then, different concentrations of ClO− aqueous solution were added. After 1 min, the solution was characterized by using fluorescence spectrophotometer, and finally fluorescence spectra (λex = 387 nm; scanning range = 400–750 nm) were obtained. Ultrapure water was used as a blank sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ClO− in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: A specific concentration of N,F-CDs was obtained with mixing different proportions of piped water sample. First, the N,F-CD solution was injected into 7-mL PCR tubes. Then, different concentrations of ClO− aqueous solution were added. After 1 min, the solution was characterized by using fluorescence spectrophotometer, and finally fluorescence spectra (λex = 387 nm; scanning range = 400–750 nm) were obtained.

Based on one-pot hydrothermal treatment, the N,F-CDs were synthesized with a mixture of 4-fluorophthalic acid and glycine in water. After synthesis, the N,F-CDs were characterized. As shown in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images in Fig. 1 A, uniform spherical particles with no significant agglomeration were obtained. The sizes of the N,F-CDs ranged from 1.5 to 4.5 nm, with an average size of approximately 3.32 nm (Fig. 1B). Fig. 1C shows the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the synthesized N,F-CDs. The characteristic peaks at 3180, 2900, 1610, and 1415 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH, the bending vibrations of NH, the asymmetric stretching vibrations of CO, and the bending vibrations of CN, respectively [29]. In addition, the two absorption peaks at 889 and 1116 cm−1 correspond to the aromatic CF stretching vibrations.

The elemental composition of the N,F-CDs was investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2 A, four peaks were obtained, corresponding to C1s (285 eV, 57.41%), N1s (401 eV, 8.25%), O1s (532 eV, 29.93%), and F1s (687 eV, 4.41%), confirming the incorporation of nitrogen and fluorine into the carbon matrix. The C1s spectrum can be further deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.78, 286.38, 288.18, and 289.08 eV (Fig. 2B), corresponding to CC/CH, CO/CN, CO, and CF, respectively. The N1s spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.78 and 401.08 eV, corresponding to C-N and N-H (Fig. 2C). The O1s spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks, corresponding to CO (531.28 eV) and CO (532.88 eV) (Fig. 2D). Deconvolution of the F1s spectrum revealed the presence of one types of fluorine bonds: covalent C-F (686.88 eV) (Fig. 2E) [30,31]. The results are consistent with the FT-IR results and show that the N,F-CDs are doped with N and F and contain functional groups such as NH2, CO and OH on the surface.

The fluorometric properties of the N,F-CDs were analyzed. Fig. 3 A shown 3D fluorescence spectra obtained at different excitation and emission wavelengths. We found that the optimal excitation and emission wavelength regions are 365–420 and 510–585 nm, respectively. The results indicate that the emission wavelength of the N,F-CDs is adjustable. In Fig. 3B, because of the π-π* electronic transitions, the UV absorption peak at 358–374 nm can be found, which can lead to strong fluorescence [32]. For subsequent experiments, we chose 387 and 545 nm as optimal excitation and emission wavelength, respectively (Fig. 3B).

To explore the fluorescence stability, the variation in the fluorescence intensity versus time of the N,F-CDs was investigated. In Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity was almost unchanged between 0 and 60 min, indicating good stability. In addition, the effect of salt concentration was also considered. As the concentration of sodium chloride increased (0–200 μM), the fluorescence intensity of the N,F-CDs dropped only slightly, but the change over the studied concentration range was small (Fig. 4B). Thus, the synthesized N,F-CDs possess good time and salt stability, suggesting that they are suitable for ion detection.

To evaluate the selectivity of the N,F-CDs for ClO−, interfering ions (e.g., Na+, Mn2+, SO3 2−, H2PO4 −, NO3 −, Zn2+, F−, Cl−, Br−, Cr6+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Ag+, H2PO2 −, Ni2+, Ca2+, K+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ac−, SO4 2−and CO3 2−) were introduced. First, compared with the fluorescence quenching effect of ClO− (40.0 µM), the interfering ions (2.0 mM) added to the N,F-CD solution alone showed only negligible quenching effects (Fig. 4C). Meanwhile, we added interfering ions to the solution of the N,S-CDs and ClO− (Fig. 4D). After the addition of interfering ions, there were no significant changes in the fluorescence quenching effect. Thus, the N,F-CDs are highly selective for ClO−, indicating that they could be used for the analysis of real samples and complex matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the N,F-CD-based fluorometric determination system
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure fluorometric stability during the test period, the dynamic response characteristic of the N,F-CD-based fluorometric determination system was further optimized (Fig. 5 A). When ClO− was added to the N,F-CD solution, the fluorescence was quenched within 1 min, and the intensity at 545 nm was decreased by approximately 99.6%. After 60 min, the fluorescence intensity remains unchanged. Thus, we selected 1 min as the fluorescence quenching time.

To ensure influence of pH during the test period, different pH of the N,F-CD-based fluorometric determination system was also further optimized (Fig. 5B). In the presence of ClO−, N,F-CD solutions of different pH value were all quenched, and there was no difference in the fluorescence intensity. In contrast, the N,F-CD solution itself was influenced by pH. When pH was increased from 1 to 4, the fluorescence intensity increased sharply. However, there was no significant change from pH 4 to 13 in fluorescence intensity. When the pH value was increased to 14, the fluorescence intensity decreased markedly. Considering the difference in the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of ClO−, pH 6 was chosen for subsequent ClO− detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ClO− based on the N,F-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: When the ions were added to the N,F-CD solution, the interaction of N and O with ions on the N,F-CD surface inhibited the n → π* transition, resulting in a significant reduction in the fluorescence intensity of the N,F-CDs. Therefore, the fluorescence intensities of the N,F-CDs with ClO− ions at different concentrations was investigated (Fig. 5C). In the range of 0–35 μM, the concentration of ClO− and the intensity presented a good linear correlation (R 2 = 0.9940) (Fig. 5D), and the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 9.6 nM, which is comparable to that of some previously reported fluorescent probes [33–37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ClO− in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent specificity combined with the high sensitivity and selectivity of the N,F-CDs toward ClO− indicate that the probe has promising potential for ClO− detection in real water samples. Thus, we carried out an assay for the detection of ClO− in real water samples. As listed in Table 1 , ClO− solutions with concentrations of 0.10, 0.50, or 1.00 μM were added to piped water sample 1; for these concentrations, recovery rates were calculated to be 102.6%, 95.1%, and 102.6%, respectively, and the relative standard deviations were 3.24%, 4.22%, and 2.07%, respectively. For piped water sample 2, ClO− solutions with concentrations of 0.50, 1.00, and 1.50 μM were added; the recovery rates were calculated to be 105.0%, 97.6%, and 95.5%, respectively, and the relative standard deviations were calculated to be 3.78, 2.64%, and 1.52%, respectively. For sample 3, ClO− solutions with concentrations of 2.00, 4.00, and 6.00 μM were added; the recovery rates were calculated to be 98.8%, 100.2%, and 100.5%, respectively, and the relative standard deviations were calculated to be 3.97, 2.05%, and 4.23%, respectively. Thus, the N,F-CDs exhibit excellent recovery and accuracy and have great potential for the quantitative detection of ClO− ions in real water samples.

In summary, we synthesized nitrogen–fluorine co-doped carbon dots (N,F-CDs) from 4-fluorophthalic acid and glycine by a facile one-pot solvothermal process. The synthesized N,F-CDs have good water solubility and dispersibility, and they exhibited good fluorescence. In the presence of ClO−, the fluorescence of the N,F-CDs was selectively quenched via the inhibition of the n → π* transition. Thus, the use of N,F-CDs is a facile, inexpensive, and fast fluorometric method for ClO− detection. This strategy is environment friendly and is promising for the detection of ClO− in real water samples.

Yujing Nie: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Investigation, Resources. Shuhan Wang: Data curation. Youxiu Lin: Data curation. Wenqiang Lai: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Wen Weng: Validation, Supervision. Dianping Tang: Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21904056), the National Science Foundation of Fujian Province (Nos. 2017J01420, 2018H0030, 2018J05022), the Education Bureau of Fujian Province (Nos. JZ160453).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Major degradation pathway of thiuram in tap water processed by oxidation with sodium hypochlorite

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New insights into the stability of silver sulfide nanoparticles in surface water: dissolution through hypochlorite oxidation

Lin, Preparation of an efficient ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe (m-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+) for visual and specific detection of hypochlorite on site and in living cells, ACS Sens. 2 (2017) 1684-1691.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective determination of free dissolved chlorine using nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe
